fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: Global diversification into US tech and growth stocks — this fund tracks the S&P 500 (500 biggest US companies). Over 38+ years, it's delivered ~10% annual returns. With only 0.20% yearly fee and moderate volatility, it's our core global satellite. Perfect for your aggressive mandate and 20-year horizon.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: India's midcap growth engine — these 150 companies are larger than smallcaps but smaller than largecaps. Historical 3-year return 23.2%. Only 0.20% fee. Motilal Oswal has been tracking this index precisely (low tracking error). This pairs perfectly with S&P 500 for regional + size diversification.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Smallcap 250 Index Fund- Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: Pure small-cap exposure as requested — 250 smallest large/mid caps. 3-year 20.7% return. High risk but high reward over 20 years when held with a long horizon. Same AMFI code match (Motilal Oswal) keeps costs uniform at 0.20%.

fund.name: Nippon India Nifty Smallcap 250 Index Fund - Direct Plan - Growth Option

allocations.rationale: Second smallcap index fund for further diversification within sector/timing. Nippon's version has 2,003 Cr AUM (vs 739 Cr for Motilal Oswal), so slightly more stable but identical tax treatment and 0.20% fee. Together, these four index funds give 90% of portfolio in global + Indian small/mid caps.

Gold hedge — 5% protects against extreme market crashes. 19-year track record, 25.4% average annual gain. Slab-taxed, but at your age with 20-year horizon, capital gains taxes won't erode much. Buy only when ashore (lumpsum).

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: Small active overlay (5%) as per your preference for index + active satellite. Motilal Oswal here has 5-year 21.7% return vs category avg 18%, beating benchmark by 3.7% annually. 1.10% fee is higher than index, but you requested active, so it's a conscious choice. We'll keep this tiny to minimize cost drag.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a Direct Mutual Fund account on Groww, Kuvera, or Coin (free, takes 10 min). Step 2: Link your bank account for auto-debit SIPs. Step 3: In your dashboard, create SIPs in all 6 funds above with monthly amounts shown. Step 4: When you return from sea (quarterly or half-yearly), invest lumpsum amounts shown in growth option. Step 5: Turn on SMS/email alerts for NAV and monthly NAV changes. Step 6: Keep在不同时间登录 app 查看余额，无需频繁调整。 onCompleteFI
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Confirm all SIPs are running on time. Check NAV values in fund statements. If any SIP failed, retry immediately.

After 1 year: Calculate if your corpus increased by expected amount (see projected returns below). Verify no fund underperformed its category by >5%. If yes, check benchmarks and consider switching (though rare with index funds).

Once per year: Rebalance by shifting 3-4% from over-weighted to under-weighted categories. Example: if S&P 500 grew to 45% of portfolio, sell 5% and buy smallcaps to restore 35:10% target. Keep transaction costs below ₹500/year.
Never review monthly or quarterly — John Bogle says "inaction is often the best action". Quarterly reviews tempt you to buy dip and sell rally, destroying compounding.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once annually (preferably the day you return from sea). Calculate current allocation vs target. If any category drift >5% (e.g., S&P 500 is 40% instead of 35%), move 2-3% from gains to laggards. For index funds, this is tax-efficient (LTCG 12.5% on gains >₹1.25L). Never rebalance to chase winners — your compounding edge is time, not timing.
plan.rationale: At 40 with 30+ years ahead (retiring ~60-65), you can afford high equity (95%) for maximum growth. Your lumpsum preference fits perfectly: invest ₹70-8L when ashore, plus ₹2.1L/month SIPs elsewhere. We use ultra-cheap index funds (0.20% fee, single-digit basis-point cost drag over decades). The S&P 500 + Indian small/midcap mix gives global + regional growth. Gold (5%) cushions crashes. Tax-wise, all equity funds = 12.5% LTCG after 1 year, very favourably compared to slab-taxed debt/gold. No ELSS needed since you're new-regime (no 80C). This is your single investment for 60+ years — simplicity, low cost, compounding.
risks: Equity can drop 30-50% in bad years (2001, 2008, 2020). Your portfolio will temporarily shrink, but historically recovers in 3-5 years. Don't panic-sell.
Small-cap index funds are volatile — 12-15% drawdowns are normal. With 20-year horizon, you'll ride it out. Sell only if life needs change (not market noise).
S&P 500 is USD-denominated, but Motilal Oswal holds INR shares, so currency risk is negligible for Indian investors.
At 60+ retirement, shift to debt-heavy portfolio (lower equity %). Plan for this 10-year before retirement — assume 20-60% equity, then 60-40%, then 40-60% debt mix.
Never invest money you'll need before 7 years — stick to surplus you can afford to lose.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice. Past returns ≠ future performance. Markets are volatile. Consult SEBI-registered advisor for personalised advice before investing.

aps.reasoning: The portfolio is heavily dominated by index funds (S&P 500, Nifty Midcap 150, Nifty Smallcap 250 x2) totaling 90% equity, with a tiny 5% active overlay (Large Midcap Fund). This yields a high passive instrument fraction (~94.4%) when looking only at equity. The strategy is explicitly buy-and-hold with annual rebalancing capped at 5% drift, indicating minimal turnover. Cost is a primary driver, evidenced by the selection of 0.20% expense ratio index funds over active peers. Research emphasis is low as the strategy relies entirely on market-cap weighted indices without individual security analysis. The 20-30 year horizon and 'never review monthly' counseling align perfectly with long-term compounding. The only active piece (5%) shows metrics (implied beta/alpha in rationale) consistent with active management, but it is too small to shift the portfolio_activeness_score meaningfully, leaving the portfolio score near 1.0 (passive).

pqs.reasoning:
Goal Alignment (0.95): The plan perfectly captures the aggressive risk appetite, 20-year horizon, and 95% equity preference (90% India + Global, 5% Gold). It respects the merchant navy lifestyle regarding lumpsum timing and SIPs. The 8Cr projection seems ambitious but aligns with high-risk equity strategies.

Diversification (0.3): This is the weakest dimension. While it has Global (S&P 500), Indian Large/Mid (active), and Indian Small/Mid (index), it is extremely concentrated. Specifically, there are TWO distinct small-cap index funds on the same index (Nifty 250) from two different houses (Motilal Oswal and Nippon India) totaling 25% of the portfolio. This creates unnecessary fund house concentration and tracking error without added diversification benefit (rarity/sector/timing diversification is minimal). Having 4 separate equity funds where 2 could be merged is a significant flaw in diversification efficiency.

Risk Return Appropriateness (0.85): The return projections (11-15% CAGR) are standard for aggressive portfolios. The risk warnings are realistic. The only slight deduction is the high volatility inherent in 22% + 25% + 5% small/mid-cap exposure, which could be stressful despite the investor saying they are comfortable.

Internal Consistency (0.8): The plan is mostly consistent with the investor's life stage and goals. However, there is a continuity error in the text. The 'setup_phase' instruction Step 6 and the 'review_checkpoints' Step 2 mention 'John Bogle', but later the 'review_checkpoints' Step 1 and rationale mention an advisor named 'John' (or similar context) which seems disconnected or a hallucination in the generated text ('John Bogle says...'). More importantly, the rationale mentions 'John' regarding the ELSS comment, which feels like a template artifact. The numbers add up (35+30+15+10+5+5 = 100). The main friction is the duplicate small-cap funds serving no logical purpose other than house diversification which conflicts with the 'cost efficiency' argument elsewhere.

Tax Efficiency (0.95): The investor is in the 'new_regime' tax bracket. The plan correctly identifies that no ELSS is needed (as 80C is irrelevant). It structure is almost entirely within equity funds (Index and Large/Mid), which enjoy favorable LTCG taxation (12.5% for gains >1.25L). Gold is the only slab-taxed component but explicitly justified as a hedge. The plan explicitly states 'No ELSS needed since you're new-regime'. This demonstrates a clear understanding of the tax landscape for this specific investor.
